main_sections.title: Travel
main_sections.poem_text:
The railroad track is miles away,
    And the day is loud with voices speaking,
Yet there isn't a train goes by all day
    But I hear its whistle shrieking.

All night there isn't a train goes by,
    Though the night is still for sleep and dreaming,
But I see its cinders red on the sky,
    And hear its engine steaming.

My heart is warm with the friends I make,
    And better friends I'll not be knowing;
Yet there isn't a train I wouldn't take,
    No matter where it's going.
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Edna St. Vincent Millay's 'Travel' is a lyric poem in three stanzas that captures the speaker's restless, irresistible longing for travel and movement. The railroad — distant yet ever-present in imagination — becomes a symbol of freedom, opportunity, and connection. The poem's central tension lies in the contrast between the noisy, crowded day and the silent, dreaming night, yet in both states the speaker is haunted by the train: its whistle shrieking by day, its cinders glowing red by night. The final stanza broadens the poem from longing to love — for friends made along the way — and resolves in a joyful, unconditional acceptance of wherever the journey leads. The dominant tone is warm, enthusiastic, and content. The ABAB rhyme scheme drives the poem's forward momentum, and personification ('whistle shrieking') and imagery ('cinders red on the sky', 'day is loud') are the chief literary devices.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before reading the poem. (I) Complete the following word web on travel (Modes of Transport, Reasons for Travel, Adventure Activities, Feelings, Destinations, Challenges). (II) You are going on a trip to an adventure camp — discuss questions 1–5 with classmates. (III) Discuss in pairs: What might be the reasons that people enjoy train journeys?

question_bank.stem: Complete the word web on travel: Modes of Transport (bus,...), Reasons for Travel (work,...), Adventure Activities (exploring,...), Feelings (joy,...), Destinations (riverside,...), Challenges (bad weather,...).

You are going on a trip to an adventure camp: (1) How will you travel to the adventure camp? (2) What things will you take along? (3) Why would you like to go there? (4) What are the activities you would like to do with your friends? (5) How much time will it take to complete your adventure trip?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — the poem. Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: The main ideas of each stanza of the poem are given below. Write the number of the stanza against the matching main idea. (1) At night, the speaker imagines the train and hears its engine in the quiet. (2) The speaker feels a strong desire to take any train, no matter where it goes. (3) The speaker hears the train whistle during the day, even though the train is far away.)
question_bank.table:
Main Idea|Stanza Number
At night, the speaker imagines the train and hears its engine in the quiet.|
The speaker feels a strong desire to take any train, no matter where it goes.|
The speaker hears the train whistle during the day, even though the train is far away.|

question_bank.stem: Read the poem and underline the rhyming words in each stanza. Write the rhyming words from each stanza. (Example — Stanza i: away-day. Stanza ii: ? Stanza iii: ?)
Identify the rhyme scheme of the poem from the options given below: (1) AABB (2) ABCA (3) ABAB (4) ABBC
Identify the word in the poem that personifies the whistle of the train.

Tone refers to the attitude the poet has about the poem. Which of these words can be used to describe the poet's tone in this poem? (serious, excited, curious, worried, thoughtful, content, enthusiastic, critical, joyful)
Select the words that tell us what the train symbolises for the poet: (1) education (2) adventure (3) comfort (4) opportunities

The poet repeats the line '...there isn't a train goes by,' in all three stanzas to _______. (highlight the speaker's fascination with the train / suggest that the area has many trains passing through)
options: highlight the speaker's fascination with the train
suggest that the area has many trains passing through

question_bank.stem: The poem contrasts the busy day with the stillness of the night. What is the common factor in these?

Imagery is a literary device that uses descriptive language to create mental pictures and appeal to the reader's senses. Select the phrases from the poem that are examples of imagery. (One example given: 'day is loud'.)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — Section I. Read the extract ('My heart is warm with the friends I make, / And better friends I'll not be knowing...') and answer the questions. Section II — Answer questions on the full poem.

question_bank.stem: The line 'My heart is warm' refers to a heart that is ___. A. filled with worries B. full of love C. feeling upset D. experiencing anger
What does the speaker say about her current friends?
What does the line 'better friends I'll not be knowing' suggest about the speaker's feelings toward their current friends?
Complete the following suitably. The tone of the speaker in these lines is ______. (grateful / content)
What does the phrase 'cinders red on the sky' tell us about the train at night?
What is the significance of the repeated references to the train's whistle?
What does the line 'no matter where it's going' reveal about the speaker's attitude towards travel?
Do you think the poet actually sees and hears the train? Why or why not?

listening.transcript_text: ANNOUNCEMENT (School Trip to National Science Centre): Good morning students! I have some very exciting news to share with you all. The much-awaited educational trip to the National Science Centre will be held on 16 September, that is in two weeks! As you know this would be a fantastic opportunity to explore and learn more about the world around us. We will be starting from school at 8.00 in the morning and return by 5.30 in the evening. All of you must be in proper school uniform. Don't forget to carry your identity cards. Light refreshments will be provided at the museum. But do carry a water bottle and a packed lunch. You are not permitted to wear any jewellery or carry mobile phones. You may carry a notebook and a pen to make notes. Interested students must bring a permission slip signed by your parent and submit them to their class teachers by 10 September. It is going to be a fun trip so don't miss out. If you have any more questions, please contact Mr. Mukherjee, the excursion in-charge.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Listen to this announcement made in the morning assembly about a school trip to the National Science Centre. As you listen, circle the information that is not correct from statements 1–7.

question_bank.stem: Listen and identify which of these statements are NOT correct: (1) The trip to the National Science Centre is organised on 15 September. (2) Students will start from school at 8.00 in the morning and return by 5.00 in the evening. (3) Refreshment will not be provided during the trip. (4) Students are permitted to carry a phone, a notebook, and a pen. (5) The permission slip is to be signed by the class teacher. (6) The permission slip is to be submitted to your class teacher by 10 September. (7) Mr. Mukherjee is the Adventure Club in-charge.
Now, listen to the announcement again and rectify the sentences (write the correct version of each incorrect statement).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: When speaking in English, small differences in pronunciation can change the overall meaning of words. The letter 'c' has two sounds — /k/ (hard c, before a, o, u or consonant) and /s/ (soft c, before e, i, y). Work in pairs: pronounce the given words aloud keeping the rules of soft and hard 'c' pronunciation in mind. (Words: cell, cable, race, cake, decide, receive, concert, circle, bicycle, echo, uncle.)

question_bank.stem: Pronounce the following words aloud and classify them as hard 'c' (/k/) or soft 'c' (/s/): cell, cable, race, cake, decide, receive, concert, circle, bicycle, echo, uncle.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: A diamante poem is a poem shaped like a diamond that uses specific types of words like adjectives and –ing words. Read the example (Day / Bright, sunny / Dancing, playing, running / Out comes the sun, down goes the sun / Resting, listening, dreaming / Quiet, peaceful / Night). Now write a diamante poem that connects 'reading' and 'adventure' using the provided template. Share your poem with your classmates and the teacher.

We come across many interesting characters while travelling. Describe one such character that you met. Follow these steps: (1) Choose the character. (2) Create a first draft of your description including: (i) What is the name of the character? (ii) What does the character look like? (iii) What does the character do? (iv) What kind of person is she or he? (v) Include any line or incident from the text that you like the most. (3) Check your description for any spelling mistakes or errors. (4) Show your classmates your work and ask for suggestions. (5) Rewrite your description including all the changes and suggestions for improvement.

Read the expressions from the poem given in Column 1 and match them with what they indicate in Column 2 (miles away, cinders red, heart is warm, day is loud, engine steaming). Then use the Column 1 expressions to fill in the following blanks correctly.

question_bank.stem: Match the poetic expressions with their meanings: (1) miles away — (i) the speaker's affection for friends / (ii) fast, powerful movement / (iii) busy, noisy atmosphere / (iv) glow of sparks lighting the night sky / (v) distance of train from speaker; 2) cinders red; 3) heart is warm; 4) day is loud; 5) engine steaming.
question_bank.table:
Expression|Meaning
miles away|The distance of the train from the speaker
cinders red|The glow of the train's sparks lighting up the night sky
heart is warm|The speaker's affection for their friends
day is loud|The busy and noisy atmosphere during the day
engine steaming|The fast and powerful movement of the train

question_bank.stem: Use the expressions (miles away, heart is warm, engine steaming, cinders red, day is loud) to fill in the blanks: (1) After meeting her old friend, her ______ with the joy of rekindled memories. (2) As I arrived at the railway station I could hear the ______. (3) The marketplace is bustling, and the ______ with the chatter of shoppers and vendors. (4) The bonfire glowed brightly, throwing ______ into the dark night sky. (5) The sound of a train could still be heard _____ as the evening grew quiet.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The poet uses 'shrieking' to tell us about the sound of a train's whistle. There are other words that tell us about different types of sounds. Match meanings in Column 1 with words in Column 2 (wailing, hollering, screeching, yelling, whimpering, bawling, screaming). Then complete the paragraph using suitable words from Column 2.

question_bank.stem: Match each meaning with the correct sound word: (1) shouting loudly, often in anger or excitement → (i) wailing / (ii) hollering; (2) crying out in fear or excitement → (iii) screeching; (3) crying loudly with grief or sorrow → (iv) yelling; (4) making a high-pitched, piercing sound → (v) whimpering; (5) calling out loudly, often in a casual or informal way → (vi) bawling; (6) crying softly in a low, broken voice → (vii) screaming; (7) crying loudly and uncontrollably.
question_bank.table:
Meaning|Word
shouting loudly, often in anger or excitement|yelling
crying out in fear or excitement|screaming
crying loudly with grief or sorrow|wailing
making a high-pitched, piercing sound|screeching
calling out loudly, often in a casual or informal way|hollering
crying softly in a low, broken voice|whimpering
crying loudly and uncontrollably|bawling

question_bank.stem: Complete the paragraph using suitable sound words: 'The street was filled with a variety of sounds. A shopkeeper was 1. ______ to attract customers, while a child nearby was 2. _______ at his friend to wait for him. From the other side of the road, a toddler was 3. ________ uncontrollably after dropping his ice cream, and his mother tried to calm him as he began 4. _________  softly. In the background, someone's loud 5. _________  voice added to the bustling noise of the busy evening.'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The Mountain Railways of India (Darjeeling Himalayan Railway, Nilgiri Mountain Railway, and Kalka–Shimla Railway) are UNESCO World Heritage Sites. The Matheran Hill Railway and Kangra Valley Railway are on the tentative list. In groups of four, find out more about these heritage trains and make a presentation in the class.

question_bank.stem: In groups of four, find out more about the Mountain Railways of India (Darjeeling Himalayan Railway, Nilgiri Mountain Railway, Kalka–Shimla Railway, Matheran Hill Railway, Kangra Valley Railway) and make a presentation in class.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The Indian Railways launched India's first indigenous semi-high-speed train, Vande Bharat Express, on 15 February 2019, on the New Delhi–Kanpur–Allahabad–Varanasi route running at 160 km/h. India's first underwater metro route in Kolkata started in March 2024 under the Hooghly River. Some of the underwater rail tunnels globally are the Thames Tunnel, Seikan Tunnel, and Channel Tunnel. In groups of four, find out more about these and make a presentation in class. Discuss which of these trains would be adventurous to travel in.

question_bank.stem: Find out more about the Vande Bharat Express, Kolkata underwater metro, and major global underwater rail tunnels. In groups of four, make a presentation in class. Discuss which of these trains would be the most adventurous to travel in.
